title: STA258
text:
The Big Question

How can we say something true aboutmillions of people we’ve never met?




Pollsters call ~1,000 Canadians and predict an election for 40 million. Doctors test a drug on 500 patients and recommend it for everyone. Netflix watches a sample of users and decides what shows to make.




That gap between “what we measured” and “what we want to know” — that’s what this whole course is about.

title: STA258
text:
Roadmap


1.1 Foundations

Statistics, population, sample
Unit, observation, variable
Parameter vs statistic
Descriptive vs inferential


1.2 Types of Data

Quantitative vs qualitative
Discrete vs continuous
Nominal vs ordinal

1.3 Inference Preview

Sampling, bias, study types

title: STA258
text:
1.1 Foundations

Definition 1.1 — Statistics The science of collecting, classifying, summarizing, analyzing, and interpreting data.




Think of statistics as a pipeline:  Question → Data → Analysis → Decision


The five verbs in the definition map roughly to the chapters of the course.

title: STA258
text:
Population

Definition 1.2 — Population The entire group of individuals, items, or measurements that share a characteristic of interest and about which conclusions are to be drawn.




Examples:

All students at the University of Toronto
All residents of Mississauga
Every possible hand in a game of poker (hypothetical!)
Every battery a factory will ever produce





A population can be real or hypothetical, finite or essentially infinite. What matters is that it’s the group you actually want to learn about.

title: STA258
text:
Sample

Definition 1.3 — Sample A subset selected from the population.



Examples:

50 randomly selected U of T students
100 randomly selected Mississauga residents
30 batteries pulled off the production line today




Why sample? Measuring everyone is usually too slow, too expensive, or simply impossible.

title: STA258
text:
Visualizing Population vs Sample







Hover over any dot to see which unit it is. Grey = population, teal = the sample.

title: STA258
text:
Your Turn: Identify Them

A researcher wants to know the average sleep of all UTM students. She randomly surveys 200 UTM students and finds they sleep 6.8 hours on average.  What is the population? What is the sample?



Population: All UTM students. Sample: The 200 surveyed UTM students.


Pause and have students answer aloud before revealing.

title: STA258
text:
Unit, Observation, Variable

Unit — the smallest entity from which data are collected (one row).


Observation — the full set of measured values for one unit.


Variable — a characteristic that varies from unit to unit (one column).

title: STA258
text:
Reading a Data Table



Student
Age
Program
Study Hours
On Campus




1
19
Statistics
20
Yes


2
22
Computer Science
15
No


3
20
Biology
28
Yes


4
21
Psychology
18
No






Each row is one observation (one student)
Each column is one variable
The unit is a single student

title: STA258
text:
Try It in R









Run it. Then change a value or add another student.

title: STA258
text:
Population Unit vs Sample Unit



Population unit One member of the entire population.


One U of T student, when the population is all U of T students.



Sample unit One member of the selected sample.


One of the 50 students you actually surveyed.




Every sample unit is also a population unit — but not the other way around.

title: STA258
text:
Parameter vs Statistic



Parameter A number that describes the population. Usually unknown.



Statistic A number that describes the sample. Calculated from data — known.




Memory trick:   Parameter ↔︎ Population  |  Statistic ↔︎ Sample  (both start with the same letter).

title: STA258
text:
Notation You Need to Memorize



Quantity
Population (parameter)
Sample (statistic)




Mean
\(\mu\)
\(\bar{x}\)


Standard deviation
\(\sigma\)
\(s\)


Proportion
\(p\)
\(\hat{p}\)


Size
\(N\)
\(n\)





Greek letters → population.   Roman letters (or hats) → sample.


Stress this. Most algebra errors later in the course come from mixing \(p\) with \(\hat{p}\) or \(\sigma\) with \(s\).

title: STA258
text:
Quick Check

For each, decide: parameter or statistic?


The average height of all NBA players.


63% of 500 surveyed Canadians support a policy.


The sample mean GPA of 80 UTM students is 3.12.


The standard deviation of grades for every student who has ever taken STA258.





1 — parameter (all NBA players) 2 — statistic (500 surveyed) 3 — statistic (80 students) 4 — parameter (every student ever)

title: STA258
text:
Compute a Statistic Yourself
Below are weekly study hours for a sample of 10 students. Calculate the sample mean.
















Solution


mean(study_hours)   # 19.6
This is \(\bar{x}\), our estimate of the unknown population mean \(\mu\).

title: STA258
text:
Descriptive vs Inferential Statistics

Descriptive statistics Numerical and graphical methods used to summarize the data you have.


Inferential statistics Using a sample to generalize to a larger population.

title: STA258
text:
Spot the Difference


Descriptive

“The average mark in this lecture of 80 students is 72%.”  “63% of these 400 Torontonians support the plan.”


Inferential

“We estimate the average STA258 mark across all sections is around 72%.”  “We estimate ~63% of all Toronto residents support the plan.”




Tell: descriptive describes this data. Inferential makes a claim beyond the data.

title: STA258
text:
1.2 Types of Data

Quantitative data Numerical measurements where arithmetic is meaningful.


Qualitative (categorical) data Values fall into categories; arithmetic doesn’t make sense.



Trap: a number doesn’t automatically mean quantitative. Postal codes, jersey numbers, and student IDs look numeric but are categorical.

title: STA258
text:
Quantitative: Discrete vs Continuous



Discrete Countable values, often whole numbers.



Number of siblings
Courses enrolled in
Defective items in a batch




Continuous Any value in a range; measured on a scale.



Height
Time spent studying
Temperature

title: STA258
text:
Qualitative: Nominal vs Ordinal



Nominal Categories with no natural order.



Eye colour
Program of study
Primary device (phone/laptop/tablet)




Ordinal Categories with a natural order.



Self-rated health (Poor → Excellent)
Movie rating (1–5 stars)
Education level

title: STA258
text:
The Full Map

                       DATA
                        |
          +-------------+-------------+
          |                           |
    Quantitative                 Qualitative
     (numeric)                  (categorical)
          |                           |
    +-----+------+              +-----+------+
    |            |              |            |
 Discrete    Continuous     Nominal       Ordinal
 (counts)    (measured)   (no order)    (ordered)



This tree is the single most useful diagram in Chapter 1.

title: STA258
text:
Classify These
Try classifying each one. Then click for the answer.







Variable
Type




Number of pets a student owns
Quantitative, discrete


Language spoken at home
Qualitative, nominal


Backpack weight (kg)
Quantitative, continuous


Postal code
Qualitative, nominal


Classroom temperature (°C)
Quantitative, continuous


Self-rated health (Poor/Fair/Good)
Qualitative, ordinal

title: STA258
text:
Check Data Types in R
R can tell you what type each variable is. Run it, then change a value.

title: STA258
text:
1.3 Introduction to Inferential Statistics

Sample → Statistic → Estimate of Parameter

title: STA258
text:
Why Uncertainty Is Built In

Two different samples of 100 UTM students will give two different sample means. So any estimate must come with a measure of how much it might be off.



This is where the tools in later chapters come from:

Confidence intervals — a range of plausible parameter values
Hypothesis tests — checking whether data support a specific claim
Statistical power — how likely we are to detect a real effect

title: STA258
text:
See Sampling Variability









Run it 5 times. Notice the sample mean changes each time — even though the true population mean is exactly 70.

title: STA258
text:
A Closer Look at Sampling
How you collect your sample determines whether inference is honest.

Simple random sample (SRS) — every unit has an equal chance of being chosen.


Systematic — every \(k\)-th unit from an ordered list (e.g., every 50th student).


Stratified — divide into groups, sample from each group.


Cluster — randomly pick whole groups, survey everyone inside.


Convenience / Voluntary response — whoever is easy to reach or chooses to respond. ⚠ Usually biased.

title: STA258
text:
Bias: When Samples Lie

Bias — a systematic tendency for a sample to differ from the population in a particular direction.




Classic bias traps:

Selection bias — surveying gym-goers about exercise habits
Voluntary response — Instagram polls (only loud opinions answer)
Non-response bias — the people who skip the survey are different from those who answer
Coverage bias — your sampling frame misses parts of the population

title: STA258
text:
Observational vs Experimental Studies



Observational Record what’s already happening — no intervention.


Survey adults: do you take vitamins? Did you catch a cold?



Experimental Randomly assign participants to treatments, then compare.


Randomly give half the volunteers Vitamin C, half a placebo, and compare cold rates.




Only well-designed experiments can establish cause and effect.

title: STA258
text:
Chapter 1 Summary



Statistics = science of learning from data under uncertainty.


We study a sample to learn about a population.


Parameters describe populations (unknown); statistics describe samples (known).


Data are quantitative (discrete/continuous) or qualitative (nominal/ordinal).


Inference uses a sample statistic to estimate an unknown population parameter — with a measure of uncertainty.


How we sample (and whether we experiment) determines whether inference is trustworthy.

title: STA258
text:
What’s Next

Chapter 2: we move into R — the tool we’ll use to compute statistics, simulate samples, and visualize uncertainty.



End of Chapter 1 ← Back to Chapter 1

title: STA258
text:
Bonus Practice
Below are some ages of UTM students in a sample. Compute:

The sample size \(n\)
The sample mean \(\bar{x}\)
The sample standard deviation \(s\)

















Solution


n    <- length(ages)
xbar <- mean(ages)
s    <- sd(ages)
\(n = 12\), \(\bar{x} \approx 20.83\), \(s \approx 1.59\).

title: Chapter 1: Introduction
text: Slides PDF

title: Chapter 1: Introduction
text:
What you’ll learn
By the end of Chapter 1 you’ll be able to:

Distinguish a population from a sample, and identify the unit, observation, and variables in a dataset.
Tell a parameter apart from a statistic — and know which is usually known.
Classify data as quantitative (discrete or continuous) or qualitative (nominal or ordinal).
Recognize common sampling methods and the biases they introduce.
Explain the goal of statistical inference: using a sample to learn about a population, with a measure of uncertainty.

title: Chapter 1: Introduction
text:
Key ideas

Statistics in one sentence
The science of learning from data under uncertainty — moving from what we measured to what we want to know.


Population vs Sample
A population is the full group we want to learn about. A sample is the subset we actually measure. We use the sample to make claims about the population.


Parameter vs Statistic
A parameter (e.g. \(\mu\), \(p\), \(\sigma\)) summarizes the population — usually unknown. A statistic (e.g. \(\bar{x}\), \(\hat{p}\), \(s\)) summarizes the sample — known, and used to estimate the parameter.


Types of Data
Quantitative data are numeric: discrete (counts) or continuous (measured). Qualitative data are categorical: nominal (no order) or ordinal (ordered).


Inference
We collect a sample, calculate a statistic, and use it to estimate an unknown population parameter — always with a measure of uncertainty. This sets up confidence intervals, hypothesis tests, and the rest of the course.

title: Chapter 1: Introduction
text:
How to use this chapter

Open the slides above for the full walkthrough, with interactive R code blocks you can edit and run in your browser.
Use the PDF button for an offline-friendly version (interactive elements won’t run there).
Try the practice exercises from the textbook at the end of Chapter 1.

title: STA258
text:
Roadmap


Setup and Purpose

2.1 The Statistical Computing Language
2.2 Installing R and RStudio
2.2.3 Positron


Programming Basics

2.3 Programming in R
R as a Calculator
Data Structures
Functions, Histograms, and Packages

title: STA258
text:
2.1 The Statistical Computing Language

R R is a programming language used for data manipulation, statistics, graphics, and reproducible analysis.


R is useful because it can:

perform calculations on numbers, vectors, arrays, and matrices
generate high-quality graphs
use built-in functions and user-contributed packages
save analysis steps in scripts that can be reused later

title: STA258
text:
Why Use R?



Point-and-click software Often easier at first, but steps may be harder to document and repeat.



R scripts Each step is written down, so the analysis is easier to check, repeat, and modify.




Main idea: R makes statistical work reproducible, transparent, and easier to automate.

title: STA258
text:
2.2 Installing R and RStudio
Before working with R code, students need two things:



R The programming language that performs the calculations.



RStudio The interface used to write scripts, run code, view files, and inspect output.




Think of R as the engine, and RStudio as the dashboard used to control it.

title: STA258
text:
2.2.1 Installing R

Step 1 Go to the official R project website: cran.r-project.org



Choose your operating system:


Windows: Download R for Windows → base → download the installer


macOS: Download R for macOS → choose the correct .pkg file


Linux: Follow the distribution-specific instructions




After installation, R can be accessed from a terminal or through RStudio.

title: STA258
text:
Installing R: Teaching Note

Important: Students should install R before installing RStudio, because RStudio needs an R installation to run code.



The ebook screenshot shows the CRAN page where students choose their operating system and download the correct installer.

title: STA258
text:
2.2.2 Installing RStudio

RStudio RStudio is an integrated development environment designed for working with R.


It provides:

a script editor
an R console
an environment and history viewer
a file browser
plots, packages, help, viewer, and presentation panes

title: STA258
text:
RStudio Workflow

Basic workflow:


Open RStudio.


Create a new script.


Write code in the Source pane.


Run the code.


Read the output in the Console or Plots pane.





Shortcut: Use Cmd + Enter (Mac) or Ctrl + Enter (Windows) to run the current line of code.

title: STA258
text:
The Four Main RStudio Panes







Pane
Purpose




Source
Write and edit scripts or Quarto files


Console
Run commands and view immediate output


Environment / History
View saved objects and past commands


Files / Plots / Packages / Help / Viewer
Manage files, plots, packages, and documentation





The ebook shows the RStudio home screen and the layout after opening a new script.

title: STA258
text:
2.2.3 Positron

Positron Positron is another desktop environment from Posit for data science and statistical computing.


For this course, students mainly need RStudio.


Positron may be useful in future workflows, but it is not required for STA258.

title: STA258
section: 2.3 An Introduction to Programming in R
text:
2.3 An Introduction to Programming in R
R is especially useful for statistics because it is designed for:

data analysis
statistical calculations
visualization
reproducible reporting



Course goal: Students do not need to become software engineers. They need enough R to calculate, summarize, visualize, and report data clearly.

title: STA258
text:
2.3.1 R as a Powerful Calculator

R can be used like a calculator. It supports arithmetic, exponentiation, logarithms, trigonometric functions, and summary calculations.





Operation
R Code
Result




Addition
2 + 3
5


Subtraction
4 - 1
3


Multiplication
6 * 2
12


Division
8 / 2
4


Exponentiation
5^2
25

title: STA258
text:
Try It: Basic Arithmetic
Edit the numbers and run the code.

title: STA258
text:
More Arithmetic Operators



Operation
R Code
Meaning




Modulo
9 %% 4
Remainder after division


Integer division
9 %/% 4
Whole-number part of division

title: STA258
text:
Vector Operations

Vector A vector is a one-dimensional collection of values of the same type.












Operations on vectors are performed element by element.

title: STA258
text:
Math Functions in R
R has many built-in mathematical functions.



Function
Meaning
Example




sqrt()
Square root
sqrt(16)


log()
Natural log
log(10)


log10()
Log base 10
log10(100)


exp()
Exponential function
exp(1)


abs()
Absolute value
abs(-5)


round()
Round to decimal places
round(3.1415, 2)

title: STA258
text: Try It: Math Functions

title: STA258
text:
Summary Functions
Common functions for summarizing data:



Function
Meaning




mean(x)
Average


sum(x)
Total


min(x)
Minimum


max(x)
Maximum


sd(x)
Standard deviation


var(x)
Variance

title: STA258
text:
Try It: Summary Functions
Change the values in x, then rerun the code.

title: STA258
text:
Quick Practice: Calculate the Mean
Fill in the blank to calculate the mean.
















Solution


mean(x)

title: STA258
text:
2.3.2 Basic R Data Structures
The primary data structures introduced in the ebook are:



Vectors
Lists
Matrices



Arrays
Factors
Data frames




Main idea: Different data structures organize information in different ways.

title: STA258
text:
Vectors

Vector A one-dimensional sequence of values of the same type.












Vectors are the basic building blocks for many R operations.

title: STA258
text:
Lists

List A flexible structure that can store elements of different types and sizes.

title: STA258
text:
Matrices

Matrix A two-dimensional data structure with rows and columns. All elements must be the same type.

title: STA258
text:
Matrix Operations










The operator %*% is used for matrix multiplication. t() transposes a matrix.

title: STA258
text:
Arrays

Array A multi-dimensional structure that can have more than two dimensions.

title: STA258
text:
Factors

Factor A data structure used to represent categorical variables.












Factors are useful for categorical data such as gender, education level, or survey responses.

title: STA258
text:
Data Frames

Data frame A table-like structure where columns are variables and rows are observations.

title: STA258
text:
Data Structure Summary



Structure
Shape
Best Used For




Vector
One dimension
Values of the same type


List
Flexible
Mixed types or grouped information


Matrix
Two dimensions
Same-type numerical tables


Array
Multiple dimensions
Higher-dimensional data


Factor
One dimension
Categorical variables


Data frame
Rows and columns
Real datasets

title: STA258
text:
2.3.3 Using Basic Functions in R
R has many built-in functions that help calculate, transform, and summarize data.

title: STA258
text:
Function Logic

General form Most R functions use the structure function_name(input).



For example, mean(x) tells R to apply the function mean to the object x.



Common mistake: Forgetting brackets, commas, or quotation marks will cause R to return an error.

title: STA258
text:
2.3.4 Make a Histogram Using RStudio
The ebook uses the built-in faithful dataset to demonstrate a histogram.

Histogram A graph that shows the distribution of a numerical variable by grouping values into intervals called bins.

title: STA258
text:
Try It: Create a Histogram
Run the code to create a histogram of waiting times in the faithful dataset.

title: STA258
text:
Histogram Breaks and Counts
The ebook also shows how to access the numerical values behind a histogram.










breaks show the interval endpoints. counts show how many observations fall in each interval.

title: STA258
text:
Quick Practice: Histogram Variable
Change the code so it creates a histogram of eruptions instead of waiting.
















Solution


hist(faithful$eruptions)

title: STA258
text:
2.3.5 Installing and Loading Packages

Package A package is a collection of extra functions, data, and tools that extend what R can do.



Install a package once:
install.packages("ggplot2")
Load it every new R session:
library(ggplot2)

title: STA258
text:
Package Reminder

Important: You install a package only once, but you must load it with library() every time you restart RStudio.

title: STA258
text:
Chapter 2 Summary

R is used for statistics, graphics, data manipulation, and reproducible analysis
RStudio is the main interface for writing and running R code
R can be used as a calculator for arithmetic and mathematical functions
Vectors, lists, matrices, arrays, factors, and data frames each store data differently
Functions take inputs and produce outputs using the form function_name(input)
Histograms display the distribution of a numerical variable
Packages extend R’s functionality and must be loaded each session with library()

title: STA258
text:
End of Chapter 2
